quote: Were you looking at battery backups or expanding your system?
stewart_read: Rep pivots to batteries immediately after John discloses existing solar — a reasonable instinct — but skips the structured orphaned_flow sequence entirely. The schema requires: own vs. lease, company name, panel size, system working. Rep asked own-vs-lease (01:38) and company (01:51) but never asked panel size or whether the system is currently working. The system-working question is a hard-DQ trigger per the schema. Skipping it means the rep booked an appointment without confirming the lead is eligible.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep assessed from John's description (grid-tied, making payments, knows his kWh) that the system is functional and skipped the working-status question to avoid a DQ conversation. Rejected — there is no evidence of deliberate assessment; the skip appears to be an oversight, not a calculated move.
coaching_implication: Pull the orphaned_flow checklist with the rep. The system-working question is a hard-DQ gate — skipping it means the closer may arrive at a disqualified lead. Manager should confirm with Kenny whether this appointment should be held or re-screened before Wednesday.

stewart_read: John reads his monthly kWh usage (1,730 kWh) and the rep responds 'K.' — canonical capture-and-drop. The schema's bill_anchor_depth directive is explicit: capture is necessary but not sufficient. The rep needed to flip this number into emotional alignment — 1,730 kWh monthly on a grid-tied system with a battery gap is the exact argument for why a Powerwall matters. Instead, the rep moves immediately to requesting a photo of the bill. The emotional leverage of the number is never used.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep was holding the kWh number to use as leverage during the specialist pitch, not the setter call. Rejected — the rep never references the number again, and the specialist handoff contains no instruction to use it.
coaching_implication: This is the floor-wide pattern the schema flags as universal. In the 1-on-1, ask the rep: 'What does 1,730 kWh a month mean for someone whose panels are covered in ice?' The answer is the flip. Rep needs to practice the capture-and-flip sequence, not just the capture.

quote: I have to check to see if I have any doctor's appointments. I got cancer.
stewart_read: John discloses a cancer diagnosis mid-scheduling. The rep's response is 'Yeah. K. Well, let's let's get an idea of what that schedule looks like.' The schema classifies vulnerability disclosures as trust gifts — the correct move is acknowledge specifically, then bridge to value. The rep acknowledged the scheduling constraint but not the human moment. The call's emotional temperature drops here and does not recover.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep kept the call moving to avoid dwelling on a sensitive topic and making John uncomfortable. Rejected — the schema is explicit that skipping past vulnerability disclosures costs more than any single coaching gap. A brief acknowledgment ('That sounds like a lot to manage — I want to make sure this is worth your time') takes five seconds and changes the register of the call.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play the moment and ask the rep what they heard. Then ask: 'What would you say to a neighbor who told you that?' The schema's suggested bridge: acknowledge specifically + connect to why the battery appointment is worth their limited time and energy. This is not a script fix — it's a human moment the rep needs to be present for.

quote: if it involves a lot of money, I don't have no time.
stewart_read: John's cost objection is direct and linked to his health situation — he's signaling that his bandwidth for financial decisions is limited. The rep responds with 'it really is super simple and easy to make switch. It doesn't cost a ton.' This engages the objection on its own terms (cost reassurance) rather than inverting. The inversion available here: the battery's value is in what it protects against — John's panels are already covered in ice and not producing; the cost of NOT having backup is the grid dependency he's already experiencing. The rep had the material for this flip (John's own ice/snow complaint at 15:17 was already foreshadowed in the call's context) but did not use it.
alternative_hypothesis: Deferral read: rep said 'that's something that the specialist will go through tomorrow with you is what the numbers look like' — partially routing cost to the specialist. Rejected as a strategic deferral because the rep had already engaged cost directly ('doesn't cost a ton') before the partial redirect. This is reassurance followed by a redirect, not a clean deferral.
coaching_implication: The schema's objection_inversion section has the exact flip for this moment: redirect from the cost of the product to the cost of the problem the product solves. In the 1-on-1, ask the rep: 'What is John already paying for — in money or in frustration — by not having battery backup?' That answer is the reframe. No fabricated numbers needed.

quote: What I wanna do is find time for my energy specialist to give you a call and go through all the details and, you know, answer the questions you have about batteries.
stewart_read: This is a positive scope-discipline moment. After collecting the bill, the rep cleanly routes battery questions to the specialist rather than attempting to answer them herself. The universal escape hatch in the schema ('That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer') is executed in spirit if not verbatim. The rep held her lane throughout the call — no panel specs, no pricing, no technical debate.
coaching_implication: Reinforce this explicitly in the 1-on-1. The rep's scope discipline was clean on a call where the customer's existing solar knowledge could have pulled her into technical territory. Name it as a strength so it becomes a conscious habit, not an accident.

quote: The only thing bad about solar cells is since it snowed and then turned into a chunk of ice — my solar cells don't work.
stewart_read: John volunteers the exact real-world pain case for a Powerwall — his panels are non-producing due to ice coverage, leaving him grid-dependent during a weather event. This is not a complaint about solar; it is an unsolicited articulation of why battery backup has value for him specifically. The rep responds with sympathy ('My goodness. Hopefully it starts to warm up') but does not anchor this as the reason the Wednesday appointment matters. The appointment is already booked, so this is a closing-reinforcement miss rather than a booking miss — but it is the strongest value anchor in the entire call and it arrives after the close.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have judged that re-opening the value conversation after booking risked destabilizing the appointment. Partially valid — but a single sentence anchoring the appointment to John's stated pain ('That's exactly why we want to get you on with the specialist — so you have options when that happens again') reinforces rather than destabilizes.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play this moment and ask: 'What did John just tell you about why he needs a battery?' Then ask: 'Did you use it?' The rep needs to practice closing-reinforcement moves — using the customer's own words to lock the appointment's relevance. This is also the flip that was missing at 13:26: John's ice problem was the answer to his cost objection, and it arrived too late because the rep wasn't listening for it earlier.

quote: Let me have my specialist call you tomorrow. We'll say 01:30. Give you some extra buffer time. And we'll call you at this phone number.
stewart_read: The button-up is incomplete. Present: appointment time (1:30 PM Wednesday), phone number confirmed (14:34). Missing: one-hour block stated, 'is that fair' verbal handshake, no-driving/at-computer anchor, text-followup promise. The schema marks 'is that fair' as predictive of show rate and flags the no-driving/computer anchor as directly linked to no-show rate. John's health situation and unresolved cost anxiety already make this appointment fragile — the incomplete button-up compounds the risk.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that a full button-up protocol would feel bureaucratic given John's health disclosure and the conversational tone of the call. Partially valid as a tone read — but the 'is that fair' handshake and text-followup promise are low-friction and high-value regardless of tone.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, walk through the button-up checklist against this call. The rep completed 2 of 6 required elements (time confirmed, phone number confirmed). The three highest-value missing elements for this specific appointment: (1) 'is that fair' handshake — verbal commitment from John; (2) text-followup promise — gives John something tangible to hold; (3) computer/email anchor — rep mentioned email at 14:37 but did not confirm John would be at a computer, only that he gets email on his phone. A phone-only appointment with a 70+ year-old customer on a battery pitch is a no-show risk.

stewart_read: John volunteers his veteran status immediately after the cancer disclosure — this is a second identity signal in quick succession. The rep's response is 'Okay. Senior. You too.' — brief and non-specific. The veteran disclosure is a rapport moment that the rep acknowledged but did not build on. There is no schema entry for veteran-specific handling, and no fabricated claim about Ion programs should be made here. The correct move is acknowledgment + bridge to the appointment's relevance to John's situation.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have kept the response brief to avoid appearing to exploit John's identity for sales purposes. Plausible as a tone read, but a genuine acknowledgment ('Thank you for your service — let's make sure this is worth your time') takes three seconds and costs nothing.
coaching_implication: Flag for Kenny: does Ion have any veteran-specific programs, financing, or incentives? If yes, this is a missed cross-sell signal. If no, the coaching point is simpler: acknowledge the identity signal genuinely and bridge to the appointment. In the 1-on-1, ask the rep what they heard when John said 'I'm a veteran' and what they could have said that would have made John feel seen without overpromising.
